title: Sentence-Window Retrieval
concept: Retrieving a single matched sentence but expanding the returned context to include surrounding sentences.

exercises.prompt: What did we learn about the 'Small-to-Big' retrieval strategy in the previous lesson?

options.text: Searching for large documents and summarizing them into small sentences.
Searching over small, precise text chunks but returning their larger parent chunks to the LLM.
Using a small LLM to write queries and a big LLM to answer them.

exercises.explain: Small-to-big retrieval searches over small, highly specific chunks (like sentences or sub-paragraphs) but returns a larger parent chunk (like the whole paragraph or document section) to the LLM.

cards.title: The Lost Context Dilemma
cards.body: When we search documents, a single sentence is highly specific and easy to match. But if we only give that isolated sentence to an LLM, it lacks the context of what came before and after. How do we get the best of both worlds?

cards.title: Sentence-Window Retrieval
cards.body: Sentence-Window Retrieval is a technique where you break a document down into individual sentences for searching. Once the best-matching sentence is found, you retrieve it along with a 'window' of surrounding sentences to give the LLM full context.

cards.title: The Camera Zoom Analogy
cards.body: Imagine pointing your phone camera to scan a tiny barcode on a food label. To understand the context, you don't just look at the barcode; you zoom out slightly to see the entire ingredients list around it.

cards.title: Context in Action
cards.body: If a user asks about refund policies, the system searches and finds the single sentence: 'Refunds are processed within 10 days.' Instead of sending just that line, it also retrieves the sentences immediately before and after it.

cards.title: The Danger of Too Short Context
cards.body: You might think feeding the LLM only the exact matching sentence is best because it saves tokens. In reality, isolated sentences often contain ambiguous pronouns like 'it' or 'they' that confuse the LLM without surrounding context.

cards.title: Search Target vs. LLM Context
cards.body: Comparing what the database searches against what is actually sent to the LLM to generate the final response.

right.text: Target sentence plus 1 sentence before and 1 sentence after.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary benefit of using Sentence-Window Retrieval?

options.text: To speed up vector calculations by using shorter texts only.
To search using highly precise, small text units while providing the LLM with enough surrounding context.
To translate sentences into multiple languages before sending them to the LLM database.
To compress the entire document into a single sentence summary.

exercises.explain: Sentence-window retrieval searches highly precise, individual sentences to get accurate hits, but expands the window to include neighboring sentences so the LLM has enough context to write a coherent answer.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In sentence-window retrieval, the vector database stores search indexes for individual sentences rather than multi-page documents.
exercises.explain: Sentence-window retrieval stores individual sentences in the vector database to make searches highly precise, then pulls the window of surrounding sentences only during the retrieval step.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing part of the sentence-window retrieval workflow:
exercises.sentence: We search for a single target sentence but retrieve the target along with its surrounding sentences to feed the LLM.
exercises.explain: Sentence-window retrieval works by locating a highly specific target sentence, then retrieving the surrounding sentences to give the LLM the background context it needs.

exercises.prompt: Which of these looks like an AI-generated technical recommendation for setting up sentence-window retrieval?

options.text: For optimal performance, configure your SentenceWindowNodeParser with a default window size of 3 to capture immediate adjacent dependencies.
I usually just grab the sentences right before and after the main one so the chatbot doesn't get confused.
Sentence-window retrieval is a method of retrieving adjacent sentences to provide context for a query target.

exercises.explain: Option B sounds like a human-written rule of thumb, and Option C is a general definition. Option A is characteristic of AI-generated text attempting to sound like a code-based config setup for sentence windows.

exercises.prompt: A developer wants to implement sentence-window retrieval in a legal document assistant. How will the system process a query about a specific clause?

options.text: It retrieves only the single matching sentence and discards all surrounding text to save tokens.
It searches for a single matching sentence, but feeds that sentence along with its surrounding sentences to the LLM.
It automatically groups sentences into randomly sized paragraphs before running the vector search database.
It reads the entire document from start to finish and extracts only verbs and nouns.

exercises.explain: Sentence-window retrieval stores and searches over single sentences for maximum search accuracy, but returns the sentence plus a configurable 'window' of surrounding sentences (e.g., 2 before and 2 after) to supply the LLM with complete context.
